title: Producer Playbook
intro: The course teaches you to engineer Luau systems. This module covers the other half of shipping a successful game: reading live-game data, understanding how Roblox distributes games, and designing for retention. Concepts are distilled from producer practice; each card links to the official Roblox documentation where the platform describes these systems itself.
note: Supplementary module — added on request. Not part of the LuauX server curriculum and kept separate from the verified course content.

topics.title: Reading the Numbers
topics.summary: Every decision about a live game should start from its data. These are the numbers that matter and what they actually tell you.
points: Check stats before and after every change you ship — a change that doesn't move a number is a guess, not an improvement.
A single metric in isolation misleads. High visits with low retention means discovery is working but the game isn't holding anyone.
Watch trends over days and weeks, not hourly noise.

metrics.meaning: Concurrent users — how many players are in the game right now. The headline health number; peaks show your ceiling.
Total sessions ever. Grows forever, so it measures reach, not current health.
The share of new players who come back one, seven, or thirty days later. D1 is the single strongest signal that the first session worked.
How long a typical session lasts. Short sessions usually mean players run out of understandable things to do.
Players leaving in the first minutes (a bounce). Almost always an onboarding or first-impression problem, not a content problem.
How monetization performs relative to your audience size — revenue alone hides whether it comes from many small or few large purchases.

topics.title: How Discovery Works
topics.summary: Roblox decides which games to show players. You cannot trick that system — you can only feed it the signals it rewards.
points: Recommendations favor games whose players stay, return, and engage. Retention and session quality are the input; visibility is the output.
The home page, search, and the charts are different surfaces — a game can rise on one before the others.
Growth speed matters: a game climbing quickly gets tested in front of more players. That's why iteration velocity feeds discovery.
If distribution drops, the fix is almost never a marketing trick — it's improving the underlying engagement numbers.

topics.title: Idea & Market Research
topics.summary: Ideas are abundant; validated demand is rare. Research is how you tell the difference before spending months building.
points: Study the top and trending charts on a regular schedule — not to copy, but to understand what fantasies players are currently paying attention to.
Play top games deliberately, like study sessions: what do you understand in the first 30 seconds, what pulls you back, where does the money sit?
Ask whether the core fantasy has mass appeal. A niche you love is not automatically a market.
Watch what's rising off-platform too — formats and memes often arrive on Roblox after proving themselves elsewhere.

topics.title: Simplicity × Depth
topics.summary: The winning pattern on the platform: one instantly understandable core mechanic that generates endless situations.
points: A new player should understand cause and effect immediately: press this, that happens. If they have to figure the game out, most won't.
Depth should be emergent (collection, optimization, competition, randomness creating variety) — not a pile of extra features.
Much of the audience is very young. 'Easy to consume' is a design requirement, not a compromise.
Complexity is the default failure mode of skilled developers: every feature you add must survive the question 'does a nine-year-old get this in five seconds?'

topics.title: First Session & Platform UX
topics.summary: D1 retention is decided in the first minutes of the first session — on whatever device the player happens to hold.
points: Play your own game, constantly, on a fresh account — broken prompts and confusing first steps are invisible to the person who built them.
Test on mobile and console, not just desktop. Console and mobile players can be a third or more of your audience, and their input works differently.
Treat the tutorial as a live system: measure, rewrite, and re-test it repeatedly rather than shipping it once.
A short scripted intro (even a simple cutscene) can measurably cut early quits by giving new players context before they're dropped into the world.
Hunt friction relentlessly: every extra tap, unclear button, or dead moment in minute one costs real retention.

topics.title: Retention Loops & Social Flex
topics.summary: Players need a concrete reason to come back tomorrow — and a way to show others what they've built or earned.
points: Design an explicit answer to 'what do I come back for tomorrow?' — a new unlock, a next collection tier, progress that continues.
Curiosity is a loop: 'what's the next thing I could get?' outperforms raw reward size.
Most top games have a social flex element: progress that other players can see, compare, and envy inside the game.
Competition between players (stealing, raiding, rankings) turns single-player progress into a reason to defend and return.

topics.title: Clippability
topics.summary: Moments worth recording are free marketing. Design them on purpose.
points: Watch what creators actually clip from games like yours: dramatic wins, funny physics, absurd fails, big steals.
Exaggerate feedback on your game's peak moments — a ragdoll flying into the void is a clip; a number ticking up is not.
If content creators can make an entertaining video without being paid, your game markets itself.
Ask of every major mechanic: what does its best moment look like in a 20-second clip?

topics.title: Iteration Velocity
topics.summary: The loop that compounds: research → hypothesis → ship → measure → repeat. Speed through this loop is a competitive weapon.
points: Ship small changes frequently — daily patches and substantial weekly updates keep both players and the discovery system engaged.
Every change is an experiment: know beforehand which number it should move, then check whether it did.
Cross-reference multiple sources — your analytics, in-game behavior, community feedback, and competitor data — before trusting a conclusion.
Most attempts won't work. Volume of shipped experiments, not perfection per attempt, is what finds the wins.

topics.title: Team & Production
topics.summary: Execution beats ideas, and execution is a people problem: skill, speed, and clear roles.
points: The idea is a fraction of the outcome; the quality and speed of execution is most of it.
Clear roles help: someone owns building the game, someone owns data, monetization, and distribution. Both jobs are full-time mindsets.
Buying templates or outsourcing without understanding the craft teaches you nothing and shows in the product.
Monetization is a design discipline of its own — plan what players pay for and why it feels fair, don't bolt it on at the end.

topics.title: the first 60 seconds
topics.summary: A game needs a player promise before it needs more systems. This field note turns a vague opening into a small Studio experiment with observable evidence, not a retention claim.
points: 1. Write one player promise: Use one sentence: `In the first minute, the player will ___ by ___, then understand ___`. Make it specific enough to build. Example: `In the first minute, the player will activate LaunchPad, see an accepted result, then know where to go next.`
2. Build the smallest observable path: 0-10 seconds: spawn safely; the first useful object is visible.
10-30 seconds: the player performs one understandable action.
30-60 seconds: the game returns feedback and points to one next goal.

3. Test the path without inventing a metric: Run a short Studio playtest with a person who has not been coached. Record what they tried first, where they paused, what they said they expected, and whether they reached the next goal. One playtest is evidence about that session, not proof of retention, discovery, or market demand.

4. Change one hypothesis at a time: Write `I believe players miss the LaunchPad because ___`. Change one cue, replay the same path, and compare the observation. Keep the result even when it disproves your idea. Do not use pressure patterns, fake urgency, or opaque reward loops to force the outcome.

topics.title: from path to metric
topics.summary: Analytics is a way to find the next question, not a machine that proves why players acted. Instrument one meaningful path only after the server has accepted it, then decide what to investigate next.
points: 1. Start with a question: Write the uncertainty before choosing a number: `Where does a new player stop understanding the first-minute path?` A metric is useful only if its result could change your next action. `Track everything` is not a question.
2. Name a short, ordered funnel: Joined the experience.
Saw the first useful cue.
Reached the LaunchPad.
Completed the first server-accepted activation.
Reached the next stated goal.

3. Log the accepted event, not a client claim: For a real onboarding funnel, Roblox event logging runs from a server Script in a published experience: Studio playtesting cannot prove delivery. Use stable step numbers and names, and log only when the server has already accepted the action.

4. Read a drop as a lead, not a verdict: If fewer players reach a step, investigate that transition: was the cue visible, was the instruction clear, did a technical problem occur, or did the cohort differ? Compare a defined time window or cohort, change one hypothesis, and keep a guardrail such as error rate or completion of the next goal. Do not claim the change caused the result from one chart alone.

5. Leave a decision record: Record `date → question → funnel step → comparison → change → guardrail → result → next action or rollback`. This is how an analytics dashboard becomes a learning loop instead of pressure to chase a number.
topics.code:
-- Server-side: only after the validated LaunchPad action succeeds
local AnalyticsService = game:GetService("AnalyticsService")

if sessionState[player].launches == 1 then
    AnalyticsService:LogOnboardingFunnelStepEvent(
        player,
        4,
        "First accepted LaunchPad activation"
    )
end

topics.title: depth without clutter
topics.summary: Depth is not a pile of features. It is a repeatable player decision that changes with context, has a readable consequence, and can be learned without a spreadsheet.
points: 1. Name one meaningful choice: Write `When ___ happens, the player chooses between ___ and ___ because ___`. The alternatives must trade something real: safety versus speed, certain reward versus risky reward, immediate progress versus information, or solo control versus cooperation.
2. Add variation, not a second tutorial: Define the context that changes the choice: map position, opponent behavior, scarce resource, teammate need, or a new goal.
Define feedback: what tells the player their choice mattered right now?
Define failure and recovery: what is lost, what can be learned, and how does the player try again without a punishing restart?

3. Build one decision slice: Do not build a tree of systems. Put the choice, consequence, and recovery into one tiny playable loop. Ask an uncoached tester what they noticed, what they chose, and what they would try differently next time.

4. Write the evidence: In Game Forge, add the choice to Mechanics & Systems and record one uncertainty in the Experiment Ledger. A tester saying `I liked it` is useful feedback, but not proof that the system has long-term depth.

topics.title: economy pacing without pressure
topics.summary: A healthy economy makes progress legible. It maps sources, sinks, and time-to-goal before it ever tries to sell something optional.
points: 1. Draw the smallest economy: Source / What action gives the player a resource, and how often?; Sink / What useful choice spends it?; Time-to-goal / About how many normal actions reach the next meaningful choice?; Recovery / What happens after a mistake or a poor choice?; Optional value / What can be purchased without blocking the core path?

2. Test a pacing hypothesis: Example: `I believe the first useful upgrade arrives too late because players leave before they can make a second meaningful choice.` Change one rate or cost, then watch first-session completion and player understanding: not only purchase conversion.

3. Set a pressure boundary: Write down what the game will not do: false scarcity, unclear odds, misleading timers, blocked basic play, or a prompt aimed at a frustrated player. A measurable purchase is not automatically a healthy one.

4. Write the evidence: Use Game Forge’s Economy and Ethical Economy fields for the source/sink map and value promise. Use the Experiment Ledger for the pacing test, its guardrail, and the keep/revise/rollback decision.

topics.title: map layout is a player system
topics.summary: A map is not decoration around mechanics. It controls what players see, where choices happen, how risk is telegraphed, and whether a goal can be understood without a tutorial wall.
points: 1. Draw the first path: Spawn: what useful object or direction is visible immediately?
Choice: where does the player decide, and what information is present?
Consequence: where do they see the result of that decision?
Recovery: how do they re-enter the loop after a failure or detour?

2. Test one spatial hypothesis: Write `I believe players miss ___ because the cue competes with ___.` Change one path cue, sightline, landmark, or interaction distance. Observe the same first-minute route with an uncoached player.

3. Keep the claim honest: A tighter map, brighter arrow, or shorter route is not universally better. It may improve orientation while harming tension, discovery, social play, or performance. Name the trade-off and choose a guardrail.
4. Write the evidence: Put the route and friction point in Game Forge’s First-session Audit. Log the single spatial change, primary signal, and guardrail in the Experiment Ledger.

topics.title: change one thing, then prove it
topics.summary: Updates become learning only when a team can later answer what changed, for whom, against what comparison, and what it decided to do next.
points: 1. Write a decision-shaped hypothesis: Use: `For ___ cohort, changing ___ should affect ___ because ___. I will also watch ___ as a guardrail.` If the result could not change a decision, the metric is probably decoration.
2. Freeze the comparison: Define the starting window or mature cohort and the review date.
Name concurrent changes, acquisition differences, device mix, or missing data.
Change the smallest reversible thing you can reasonably isolate.

3. Close the loop: Record the observed result, alternate explanations, and one decision: keep, revise, roll back, or gather more evidence. Do not write `proved` when the evidence only made a hypothesis more or less plausible.

4. Write the evidence: Game Forge’s Experiment Ledger is the source of truth. It keeps the hypothesis, primary signal, guardrail, smallest change, result, and decision together instead of scattering them in chat messages.
